seed_nodes.statement: For a runner with positive integer speed v on the unit circle, define the 'bad set' B_v = {t in [0,1) : min({v*t}, 1 - {v*t}) < 1/n}, where {x} = x - floor(x) is the fractional part. B_v is the set of times when the runner is within angular distance 1/n of the reference position (origin). The Lebesgue measure of B_v is exactly 2/n.

The Lonely Runner Conjecture for n runners (1 fixed at origin, n-1 with distinct positive integer speeds v_1 < v_2 < ... < v_{n-1}) is equivalent to: the union of bad sets B_{v_1} union B_{v_2} union ... union B_{v_{n-1}} does not cover all of [0,1). That is, there exists a time t when every runner has distance at least 1/n from the fixed runner.

Weyl's Equidistribution Theorem: For any real alpha that is irrational, the sequence {k*alpha mod 1 : k = 1, 2, 3, ...} is equidistributed in [0,1). More generally, for distinct real numbers alpha_1, ..., alpha_m with 1, alpha_1, ..., alpha_m linearly independent over Q, the joint sequence ({k*alpha_1}, ..., {k*alpha_m}) is equidistributed on the torus [0,1)^m.

For n=2 (one runner at speed v_1): B_{v_1} has measure 2/2 = 1, but it consists of v_1 intervals each of length 2/(2*v_1) = 1/v_1, and the complementary set (the 'good times') has measure 0. Wait — for n=2, the conjecture says runner must be at distance >= 1/2 at some time; since B_{v_1} has measure 2/2=1 and does not cover [0,1) because the intervals in B_{v_1} are exactly {t: {v_1*t} < 1/2} which has measure 1/2, and the complement has measure 1/2. Correction: for the n-runner problem, measure of B_v is 2/n. For n=2: each bad set has measure 2/2=1, which already exceeds 1. But the conjecture is trivially true for n=2: at t = 1/(2*v_1), the runner is at exactly distance 1/2. For n=3: two runners at speeds v_1 < v_2; the union of two bad sets each of measure 2/3 can cover at most 4/3 > 1 by measure alone, but the conjecture is still true and can be verified combinatorially.

target.statement: The Lonely Runner Conjecture holds for n = 11: Given any 10 runners with distinct positive integer speeds v_1 < v_2 < ... < v_10 on the unit circle, with a fixed runner at the origin, there exists a time t > 0 such that every moving runner is at angular distance at least 1/11 from the fixed runner simultaneously. Equivalently, the 10 bad sets B_{v_1}, ..., B_{v_10} do not cover [0,1).

planning_constraints: Do not cite the Barajas-Serra (2008) result that LRC holds for n <= 7 as a given fact; if that result is needed, its proof must be derived from the seeds provided.
Do not use the Three-Distance Theorem (Steinhaus conjecture) as a black box; any application of it must be proved from the seed definitions.

The naive union bound gives total measure sum mu(B_i) = 2*10/11 = 20/11 > 1, which is insufficient to conclude the bad sets do not cover [0,1); an argument that exploits the arithmetic structure of the speeds is required.
Do not directly generalize a proof for n < 11 to n = 11 without a complete argument for each step of the generalization.
If a Fourier / spectral method is proposed, the key estimates must be derived; do not assume Fourier decay bounds without proof.
Do not treat the conjecture itself or any equivalent reformulation as an available intermediate fact.
